author: Justin Martyr
interpretation: Justin cites the prophecy of a king riding a donkey as evidence that the Messiah's first coming was marked by humility, not military conquest. He contrasts this with the second coming in power and glory. The donkey is the emblem of peace — a king who rides a warhorse comes to conquer, but a king who rides a donkey comes to save.
context: Justin's two-advent framework (first in humility, second in glory) resolved the apparent contradiction between messianic prophecies of suffering and sovereignty, becoming standard Christian eschatological teaching.

author: John Chrysostom
interpretation: Chrysostom reads the triumphal entry as a deliberate prophetic enactment. Jesus chose the donkey specifically to invoke Zechariah's prophecy, publicly declaring himself the promised king while simultaneously redefining kingship. The crowds understood the messianic claim but misunderstood its nature — they expected a liberator from Rome, not a savior from sin.
context: Chrysostom's emphasis on the crowd's misunderstanding became a key homiletical point for Palm Sunday preaching: genuine recognition of Jesus combined with fundamental misapprehension of his mission.

author: Thomas Aquinas
interpretation: Aquinas collects patristic opinions on the passage and adds that the donkey allegorically represents the Gentile nations — previously unbroken and untamed, now bearing Christ to Jerusalem. The colt on which no one has sat signifies humanity in its unredeemed state, made serviceable by Christ's lordship. The entry into Jerusalem prefigures Christ's entry into the heavenly Jerusalem.
context: The Catena Aurea ('golden chain') was Aquinas's compilation of patristic commentary on the Gospels. His allegorical reading of the donkey as the Gentiles drew on Origen and became widely influential in medieval preaching.

author: Martin Luther
interpretation: Luther emphasizes the paradox of a king who is simultaneously 'righteous and having salvation' yet 'lowly and riding on a donkey.' This king saves not by force but by absorbing the world's hostility. Luther contrasts the kingdom of this donkey-king with the kingdoms of the world: earthly kings take from their subjects, but this king gives everything — his righteousness, his peace, his very life.
context: Luther's Palm Sunday sermons drew sharp contrasts between the theology of glory (worldly power) and the theology of the cross (divine power hidden in weakness). Zechariah 9:9 perfectly embodied his central theological distinction.

author: John Calvin
interpretation: Calvin argues that the prophecy must be read in its full context — the king who comes in peace also brings judgment in the surrounding verses. The donkey-riding is not weakness but a statement about the nature of the kingdom: it is established by the word and Spirit, not by military force. Calvin warns against spiritualizing away the political dimensions entirely while insisting the kingdom transcends any earthly political order.
context: Calvin's balanced reading — neither purely spiritual nor purely political — reflected his broader concern with how the church relates to civil authority. The messianic kingdom is real but not identical with any human government.

author: Ernst Wilhelm Hengstenberg
interpretation: Hengstenberg argues that the specificity of Zechariah 9:9 — the donkey, the peace mandate, the universal reign — constitutes a prophetic portrait too detailed to be coincidence and too precise to be applied to any non-messianic figure. He catalogs the pre-Christian Jewish sources that recognized this passage as messianic, demonstrating that the Christian reading is not a later invention but inherited from Judaism.
context: Hengstenberg's appeal to pre-Christian Jewish messianic expectation was a powerful apologetic strategy. By showing that Jews before Jesus read these texts messianically, he undercut the critical claim that messianic readings were Christian retrojections.

author: Darrell Bock
interpretation: Bock reads the triumphal entry as Jesus's most overt public messianic claim prior to the passion. By deliberately staging the entry to match Zechariah 9:9, Jesus forced his audience to confront his identity. The acted parable declared kingship while the choice of animal declared the kind of king he would be — one whose authority rests on righteousness and peace, not coercion.
context: Bock's emphasis on Jesus's deliberate prophetic staging reflects the broader evangelical conviction that Jesus understood himself as fulfilling specific Old Testament prophecies, not that fulfillment language was imposed after the fact by the early church.
